name: hig-platforms
description: Apple Human Interface Guidelines for platform-specific design across iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, visionOS, and watchOS.
capabilities: Explain platform-specific design conventions for all six Apple platforms
Compare navigation patterns like tab bars on iOS versus sidebars on macOS
Recommend input method adaptations for touch, pointer, remote, and spatial interaction
Provide accessibility guidance tailored to each platform's capabilities
Guide app adaptation strategies when porting between platforms
Reference official Apple HIG sections for specific design questions
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 567 static analysis findings are false positives. The skill contains only markdown documentation files referencing Apple's official Human Interface Guidelines. URLs are legitimate links to developer.apple.com. Terms flagged as 'C2 keywords' and 'weak cryptographic algorithm' are standard HIG terminology in documentation context. No executable code present - purely reference documentation.
valueStatement: Building apps for multiple Apple platforms requires understanding each platform's unique conventions. This skill provides expert guidance on iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, visionOS, and watchOS design patterns to help you create native experiences users expect.
userTitle: Design Apps for Every Apple Platform
limitations: Provides design guidance only, cannot generate actual UI code or assets
References Apple HIG documentation but cannot access external URLs at runtime
Does not replace official Apple developer documentation for implementation details
Platform guidance reflects current HIG but may lag behind OS updates

useCases.title: iOS to macOS Port
useCases.description: Adapting an iPhone app to Mac requires converting tab bars to sidebars, adding menu bars and keyboard shortcuts, and supporting resizable windows with denser layouts.

useCases.title: Universal App Design
useCases.description: Building a new app for iPhone, iPad, and Mac requires understanding when to share design elements versus when to implement platform-specific navigation and interaction patterns.

useCases.title: Spatial Computing Preparation
useCases.description: Porting an existing iPad app to visionOS requires learning spatial windows, volumes, spaces, and adapting touch interactions to eye tracking and hand gestures.

promptTemplates.title: Platform Comparison
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm designing an app for iOS and macOS. What are the key differences in navigation patterns I should know about?

promptTemplates.title: Input Method Adaptation
promptTemplates.prompt: My app uses swipe gestures on iPhone. How should I adapt these interactions for macOS trackpad and iPad with pointer support?
promptTemplates.scenario: Intermediate: Adapting interactions across input methods

promptTemplates.title: Layout Translation
promptTemplates.prompt: I have a master-detail interface on iPad using Split View. What's the equivalent pattern on iPhone and macOS?

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Platform Strategy
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm launching on iOS first, then expanding to iPadOS, macOS, and visionOS. What design decisions should I make now to simplify future platform expansions?
promptTemplates.scenario: Advanced: Strategic planning for platform roadmap

bestPractices: Respect each platform's identity - do not simply replicate designs across platforms
Match input methods to interaction patterns - touch for direct manipulation, pointer for precision
Use platform-specific navigation conventions users already understand
antiPatterns: Porting iOS tab bar directly to macOS without adapting to sidebar navigation
Using touch-optimized button sizes on macOS where smaller pointer-targeted controls are expected
Ignoring platform accessibility features like Dynamic Type on iOS or VoiceOver on macOS

faq.question: Should I build a universal app or separate apps for each platform?
faq.answer: Universal apps simplify distribution and code sharing. However, each platform requires distinct UI implementations to feel native. Consider a universal app with platform-specific view controllers rather than identical UI across platforms.

faq.question: How do I handle different screen sizes on iPhone?
faq.answer: Use Auto Layout and Size Classes to adapt to all iPhone screen sizes. Design for the smallest target screen first, then ensure layouts scale appropriately. Test on iPhone SE, standard iPhone, and Pro Max sizes.

faq.question: What's the minimum OS version I should support?
faq.answer: Support at least two major OS versions back. As of 2025, iOS 17+, iPadOS 17+, macOS 14+, tvOS 17+, visionOS 1+, and watchOS 10+ cover the vast majority of active devices while enabling modern APIs.

faq.question: Do I need to redesign my app completely for each platform?
faq.answer: Not completely. Share business logic, data models, and backend services. Adapt only the UI layer to match each platform's conventions. SwiftUI enables significant code sharing while rendering platform-native interfaces.

faq.question: How important is it to follow HIG exactly?
faq.answer: HIG conventions exist because users expect them. Deviating creates friction and confusion. You can express brand identity through color, typography within guidelines, and content - but keep navigation and interaction patterns familiar.

faq.question: Can I use the same design system across all platforms?
faq.answer: Your internal design system can define shared tokens like colors and spacing. However, the component implementations must output platform-native controls. A button looks and behaves differently on iOS versus macOS - respect those differences.
